raw_text:
Operator: Good afternoon and welcome to the Apple Inc. Q4 2020 earnings conference call. At this time all participants are in a listen-only mode. After the speakers' presentation there will be a question-and-answer session. Today's call is being recorded.

I would now like to turn the call over to Investor Relations.

IR: Thank you operator and good afternoon everyone. With me on the call today are our executives. Before we begin, I'd like to remind you that today's discussion will include forward-looking statements that involve risks and uncertainties...

[Prepared remarks omitted for brevity]

Operator: Now we'll open up the call for questions.

Simeon Gutman -- Morgan Stanley -- Analyst
On the advertising roadmap — competitors have been aggressive on launches. Where are you in the cycle and what's the monetization timeline?

Luca Maestri -- Chief Financial Officer
Look, I think it's premature to draw conclusions from one quarter of data. The macro environment is dynamic — there are a lot of cross-currents — and we want to be appropriately humble about what we can and can't predict. We'll continue to monitor the situation and we'll update you as appropriate. As we've discussed, our model has historically been resilient through cycles.

Ross Gerber -- Gerber Kawasaki -- Analyst
Given the rate environment and the consumer slowdown signals, are you seeing any change in customer behavior, and how does that flow through your forward outlook?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
On capex — yes, the number was elevated, and that reflects the timing of a few large projects. We'd guide you to think of it as a high-water mark; the back half normalizes, but I don't want to give you a specific quarter. The investment is consistent with the plan we laid out.

Chuck Grom -- Gordon Haskett -- Analyst
We've seen pretty aggressive moves from JPMorgan. Are you seeing any share shift, and what's your strategy to defend in the segment?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
Look, on guidance — we've tightened the range, which I think is the most useful thing we can do. The puts and takes are roughly what you'd expect: pricing is contributing, volume is mixed, and there's some currency. We feel comfortable with where consensus is sitting.

Glenn Schorr -- Evercore ISI -- Analyst
On the balance sheet — net leverage ticked up to 3.2x. How are you thinking about the optimal capital structure, and is there a refinancing window we should be aware of?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
On capex — yes, the number was elevated, and that reflects the timing of a few large projects. We'd guide you to think of it as a high-water mark; the back half normalizes, but I don't want to give you a specific quarter. The investment is consistent with the plan we laid out.

Stacy Rasgon -- Bernstein -- Analyst
On the balance sheet — net leverage ticked up to 2.2x. How are you thinking about the optimal capital structure, and is there a refinancing window we should be aware of?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
Sure, Stacy. Gross margin came in at 41.9%, which was ahead of our internal plan by about 40 basis points. The puts were lower freight; the takes were mix shift to lower-margin geographies. Going into next quarter we expect a roughly 43-46% range, and for the year we're tightening to 43-47%. The biggest delta line by line is customer mix.

Wamsi Mohan -- Bank of America -- Analyst
Given the rate environment and the consumer slowdown signals, are you seeing any change in customer behavior, and how does that flow through your forward outlook?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
Sure, Wamsi. Gross margin came in at 43.2%, which was beat our internal plan by about 40 basis points. The puts were favorable FX; the takes were mix shift to lower-margin geographies. Going into next quarter we expect a roughly 46-48% range, and for the year we're tightening to 45-46%. The biggest delta line by line is component costs.

Doug Leggate -- Bank of America -- Analyst
Headcount was down 2% sequentially. Is this a structural reset or a pause, and what does that imply about the operating-margin glide path?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
On capex — yes, the number was elevated, and that reflects the timing of a few large projects. We'd guide you to think of it as a high-water mark; the back half normalizes, but I don't want to give you a specific quarter. The investment is consistent with the plan we laid out.

Mike Mayo -- Wells Fargo -- Analyst
On the trading platform roadmap — competitors have been aggressive on launches. Where are you in the cycle and what's the monetization timeline?

Luca Maestri -- Chief Financial Officer
Yeah, look, Mike, as we've discussed previously, we don't break out the components of gross margin at that level of granularity. What I would say is that we feel really good about the business, we feel really good about the long-term trajectory, and we'll continue to invest behind the things that matter for our customers. Over time, you should expect us to deliver, and we're confident in our ability to execute against our plan. As I mentioned in the prepared remarks, there are a lot of moving pieces in any given quarter.

Doug Anmuth -- JPMorgan -- Analyst
On the balance sheet — net leverage ticked up to 3.1x. How are you thinking about the optimal capital structure, and is there a refinancing window we should be aware of?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
Thanks Doug. The bridge is roughly half pricing — about 49% — and the rest is mix and volume in roughly equal proportion. To put a finer point on it, in the back half we've baked in single-digit volume growth and modest pricing tailwinds, and the sensitivity to a 100bp move in wages is approximately $191 of operating income.

Brian Nowak -- Morgan Stanley -- Analyst
Your full-year guidance implies a meaningful step-up in the back half. Can you walk us through the bridge — how much is pricing, how much is volume, and how much is mix?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
Thanks Brian. The bridge is roughly half pricing — about 42% — and the rest is mix and volume in roughly equal proportion. To put a finer point on it, in the back half we've baked in single-digit volume growth and modest pricing tailwinds, and the sensitivity to a 100bp move in FX is approximately $156 of operating income.

Betsy Graseck -- Morgan Stanley -- Analyst
On gross margin — you guided 46% last quarter and you printed 42.8%. What are the puts and takes for next quarter and how should we think about the trajectory into next year?

Tim Cook -- Chief Executive Officer
On capex — yes, the number was elevated, and that reflects the timing of a few large projects. We'd guide you to think of it as a high-water mark; the back half normalizes, but I don't want to give you a specific quarter. The investment is consistent with the plan we laid out.

Chuck Grom -- Gordon Haskett -- Analyst
Headcount was down 3% sequentially. Is this a structural reset or a pause, and what does that imply about the operating-margin glide path?

Luca Maestri -- Chief Financial Officer
It's a great question. Broadly speaking, the framework we use is the same framework we've always used, which is to balance investment with returns over the appropriate time horizon. I don't want to get out ahead of ourselves on guidance — we'll talk more about that on the next call. What I'd point you to is the long-term algorithm we laid out at the analyst day; that hasn't changed.

Ross Gerber -- Gerber Kawasaki -- Analyst
On the cloud roadmap — competitors have been aggressive on launches. Where are you in the cycle and what's the monetization timeline?

Luca Maestri -- Chief Financial Officer
Thanks Ross. So margin came in at 41.0%. Without getting into the line-by-line, the key drivers were a mix shift toward services and some FX tailwinds we'd called out previously. For next quarter we'd expect to be in roughly the same neighborhood, plus or minus. We're not changing the long-term framework.

Justin Post -- Bank of America -- Analyst
Given the rate environment and the consumer slowdown signals, are you seeing any change in customer behavior, and how does that flow through your forward outlook?

Luca Maestri -- Chief Financial Officer
Yeah, I appreciate the question. We don't comment on competitive dynamics in real time. What I can tell you is that we're maniacally focused on the customer, and when we focus on the customer we tend to do well. There's a lot of noise in the market and we try not to react to every data point. As I've said before, we run our own race.

Operator: This concludes today's question-and-answer session. Thank you.
